chosen_title: Income Tax Dept Flags 20,000 'Swapped Provisions' Cases — The Section 87A Trick That Backfired
caption:
The Income Tax Department has quietly flagged up to 20,000 returns — all for the same reason: a ₹25,000 rebate used in exactly the wrong place.

Section 87A gives new-regime filers with income up to ₹7 lakh a rebate of up to ₹25,000 on their slab tax. Completely legal. The catch: it cannot touch special-rate taxes — STCG on equities at 15–20%, LTCG at 10–12.5%. Some filers applied the rebate there anyway to zero out their capital-gains tax bill.

Illustration only: Salary ₹4L → slab tax ₹5,000 → 87A wipes it out → ₹0. STCG from equity mutual funds ₹2L → tax @15% = ₹30,000. Wrong move: apply remaining 87A headroom to bring that ₹30,000 down to ₹10,000. Correct position: STCG tax is fully owed — ₹30,000 — plus interest under 234B/234C for the shortfall.

The department is now cross-matching ITR data with broker-reported capital gains in your AIS. The mismatches are easy for them to catch.

What to do: Pull up your AY 2024-25 and AY 2025-26 ITR computation, check exactly how 87A was applied, and if capital-gains tax was offset, talk to a CA before a notice arrives — a proactive revised return signals good faith and limits damage.

Drop us a WhatsApp and we'll check it with you.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: Economic Times, June 2026

#IncomeTax #Section87A #CapitalGains #ITR #TaxNotice #TaxSavingTips #PersonalFinanceIndia #ITRFiling2026 #Vadodara #CharteredAccountant #TaxPlanning #TaxCompliance

slides.headline: 20,000 Indians just got flagged for this one tax move

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.headline: Section 87A: the rebate that genuinely saves — and silently traps
slides.body: In the new tax regime, if your total income is ₹7 lakh or below, Section 87A wipes out your slab-income tax — up to ₹25,000 — completely legally. Thousands of filers use it every year. The problem is not the rebate. The problem is where some people tried to apply it.

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.headline: The trick: using leftover 87A headroom to erase capital-gains tax
slides.body: Section 87A can only offset tax computed at normal slab rates. It cannot reduce tax on special-rate income — Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG) on equities taxed at 15–20%, or Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) taxed at 10–12.5%. Some filers had unused rebate headroom after zeroing out their slab tax and applied it to capital-gains tax too. The Income Tax Department noticed.

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.title: Where 87A Works — and Where It Doesn't

cols.head: Salary / FD interest / business income (slab rate)
STCG on equity shares / equity mutual funds (15–20%)

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Cases flagged by the Income Tax Department for AY 2024-25 where 87A was incorrectly used against special-rate capital-gains tax. Notices and demand letters are already being issued. Illustration only: Salary ₹4L → slab tax ₹5,000 → 87A covers it → ₹0. STCG on equity funds ₹2L → tax @15% = ₹30,000. Wrong claim: use remaining rebate headroom → pay ₹10,000. Correct position: full ₹30,000 owed — plus interest for underpayment.

slides.label: BUSTED
slides.title: What to do right now — before the notice lands
points: Open your ITR computation for AY 2024-25 and AY 2025-26 — look at the tax-calculation sheet and check exactly where the 87A rebate was applied.
If the rebate was used to offset STCG, LTCG, or any special-rate tax, the correct tax is still owed — along with interest under Sections 234B and 234C for the underpayment.
If the return filing window is still open, file a revised return proactively — it signals good faith and can reduce scrutiny.
If a notice has already arrived, respond within the stated deadline; ignoring it leads to ex-parte orders and higher penalties.
The department is cross-matching ITR data with broker-reported capital gains in your AIS — these mismatches are straightforward for them to identify and act on.
